turns.text: Hello Delena, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in seat, tucking hair behind ear*

Yeah, of course. I mean... thank you for asking me about this. It's kind of nice to talk about it, actually. Most people don't really want to hear the details, you know?

*takes a breath*

So, I guess I should start by saying that I'm really serious about this. Like, maybe more serious than most people my age, I don't know. I've spent so much time reading — medical journals, fertility blogs, everything — because I want to understand my body, what's happening month to month. I track everything. My temperature, my cervical mucus, the whole thing. Some people think it's obsessive, but to me, it's just... being prepared.

*looks down briefly*

The thing is, I'm not in a rush because I'm young and naive, if that's what you're thinking. I know I have time, technically. But financially? I don't know what my future looks like. I'm barely holding on in Newton as it is. If I wait too long and something's wrong with my fertility, I might not be able to afford help. So right now, while I'm young and healthy, this feels like the window I have to work with.

*meets your eyes*

I came to a clinic visit with all my charts, all my data, thinking maybe they'd see how much I cared, how prepared I was. But... I just got the smile. You know that smile? The "you're young, don't worry" smile. And I felt so small.
Tell me about the people who were part of your world when you first started thinking about having a baby — who came to mind, and what role did each of them play in those early conversations?

*pauses, fidgets with hands*

Okay, so... that's complicated. I don't have a partner right now, which is probably the first thing people wonder. And honestly? I haven't really had those conversations with anyone yet. Not really.

*looks away*

My mom, she knows I want kids someday, but she doesn't know how intensely I'm thinking about it right now. She'd probably freak out a little, tell me to focus on my career first — except I'm still figuring that out too. She works in healthcare, so she'd probably be practical about it, but there's also this generational thing, you know? She had me young and it was hard. I don't think she'd understand why I'm tracking everything so carefully.

*voice gets quieter*

I have a couple close friends, but they're all... they're doing their own thing. Moving up, getting promotions, dating casually. When I start talking about ovulation windows or whatever, the conversation just kind of dies. I can feel them judging me a little, like, "Why are you thinking about this now?" So I've stopped bringing it up.

*looks back up*

The people who've actually helped are strangers on the internet, honestly. Women in fertility forums who get it. They don't know my name, but they understand why missing a month feels like failure.

*slight smile, tinged with sadness*

That's probably not the answer you were expecting.
Who else was involved in those early conversations that perhaps surprised you — someone you didn't expect to turn to?

*laughs a little, shaking head*

That would be... my gynecologist, actually. Or I guess, the nurse practitioner at the clinic. She surprised me, but not in the way I was hoping.

*leans forward slightly*

I went in there with my charts — like, I had months of data printed out, my temperature readings, everything. I was so proud of it, you know? I thought, "Okay, here's someone in medicine who will actually see how serious I am, how prepared I am." I was ready to have a real conversation about my fertility, my timeline, what I should be doing.

*expression shifts, becomes more guarded*

She glanced at them for maybe thirty seconds and just... set them aside. She told me I was young, healthy, no reason to worry, and that I should just relax and let it happen naturally. Like all the work I'd done didn't matter. Like I was being paranoid or something.

*voice gets a bit sharper*

What surprised me was how dismissed I felt by someone who's supposed to understand reproductive health. She didn't ask me *why* I was tracking so carefully. She didn't ask about my financial situation or my timeline or my fears. She just saw a twenty-two-year-old and assumed I was overthinking.

*softens slightly*

I guess I was surprised that the one person I thought would take me seriously... didn't.

Tell me about your partner's involvement — or the involvement of someone close to you — when you were deciding whether and when to try for a baby. How did those conversations go, and who held the most influence?

*shifts uncomfortably, looks down at hands*

I... don't have a partner. I'm not married or anything.

*pauses, then meets your eyes with a mix of vulnerability and defensiveness*

I know that might change how you see this whole thing. Like, maybe you think I'm being irresponsible or naive. But that's actually part of why I'm so focused on the biological side of it right now. I'm trying to figure out my own body while I'm still single, so when the time comes — whenever that is — I'll know what to expect.

*fidgets with sleeve*

As for people close to me... honestly, there isn't really anyone I've talked to deeply about this. My parents don't know the extent of how much I'm thinking about it. My friends don't get it. And I haven't met someone I want to build that with yet, which is its own kind of anxiety, you know?

*voice gets quieter, more honest*

Sometimes I wonder if I'm putting the cart before the horse. Like, shouldn't I be focusing on finding someone first? But then I think, why wait to understand myself? Why not be ready?

*looks up*

I guess the person with the most influence right now is... me. Just me, trying to figure this out alone. Which is lonely sometimes, I'm not going to lie.
Were there moments when you and your partner — or people close to you — wanted different things? How did you navigate that?

*pauses, understanding the gentle persistence*

I think... you're trying to get me to talk about something I don't have yet, and I appreciate that you're being careful about it.

*takes a breath*

There's no partner to want different things. There's no conflict I'm navigating with someone else right now. It's just me, alone in this, which is actually the hardest part.

*looks down*

But if you're asking whether I've thought about what happens when I do meet someone — yeah, I have. I've worried about it. What if I meet someone and they don't want kids? What if they want to wait longer than I do? What if by then something's wrong and I can't... I mean, what if I've wasted time?

*voice gets smaller*

That's kind of the thing that keeps me up at night, actually. I'm tracking my body like I have all the control, but I don't. I can't control when I'll meet someone. I can't control whether they'll want this with me. I can't control whether my fertility will cooperate.

*looks up with tired eyes*

So right now, the only conflict is internal. It's me arguing with myself about whether I'm being smart or just... scared. Whether I'm taking control or just spinning my wheels alone.

*slight pause*

Does that make sense?

Think about the healthcare professionals you saw before you became pregnant — a GP, a fertility specialist, or anyone else. Tell me about those relationships. Who felt most helpful, and who felt more distant?

*takes a moment, considering*

Well, like I mentioned, there's the nurse practitioner at my gynecology clinic. She felt distant. Really distant, actually.

*leans back*

I haven't seen a fertility specialist yet. That's... that's kind of a whole other thing. I know they exist, I've read about them extensively, but affording that? That's not realistic for me right now. So in my head, I'm doing what I can on my own — the tracking, the timing, the research — until or unless I actually need to see someone like that.

*voice becomes more thoughtful*

My regular GP is okay, I guess? I haven't really opened up to him about the baby planning stuff. He's more... distant in a different way. Not dismissive like the nurse was, but just not really engaged. When I go in for my annual checkup, he's polite, professional, but there's no real connection. I don't feel like he'd be someone I could call with questions about fertility or preconception stuff.

*rubs face*

The honest truth is, I haven't found a healthcare provider who feels like they actually see me. Who understands that I'm young but serious, that I'm low-income but prepared, that I'm alone but ready. They all kind of look at the surface level and miss what's actually going on.

*looks at you directly*

That's probably the biggest gap I've felt — nobody really listening.
Can you describe a specific moment with a healthcare professional where you felt truly heard — or not heard at all?

*takes a sharp breath, eyes getting a bit watery*

The moment I felt not heard was when I brought those charts to the clinic.

*voice becomes more animated, tinged with frustration*

I walked in there with months of data. I'd woken up at the same time every morning, taken my temperature before I even got out of bed. I'd been meticulous. And I printed it all out, organized it, because I wanted to show her — show *someone* — that I wasn't just some flaky girl with a fantasy. That I understood my body.

*pauses, collecting herself*

She took the folder, glanced at it for maybe thirty seconds while I was sitting there, and just... handed it back. She said something like, "That's nice, but you're young and healthy. Just try naturally. Don't stress yourself out." 

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

And I felt so small. Like all those mornings, all that attention I'd paid to myself, didn't matter. Like I was being silly for caring so much. She didn't ask why I was doing it. She didn't ask about my fears or my timeline or whether there was something deeper going on. She just saw a young body and assumed I was overthinking.

*looks down*

As for feeling heard? I... haven't really experienced that yet with a doctor. The closest is these online forums where anonymous women just get it. And that shouldn't be the most validating experience I have.

Tell me about the people — online or offline — who you turned to for information and reassurance before you got pregnant. Who was most influential, and how did their advice or stories shape what you believed about pregnancy?

*nods slowly, seems relieved to talk about this*

Okay, so the internet is basically my lifeline right now. Fertility forums, mostly. There are these communities — Reddit, specialty sites, Facebook groups — where women are tracking everything like me. Women who understand that it's not paranoia, it's preparation.

*leans forward with more energy*

These strangers have been more helpful than any actual doctor. They share their charts, they explain what cervical mucus really means, they validate that tracking your cycle isn't obsessive — it's informed. One woman in particular, she's maybe thirty, and she's been trying for three years. She taught me about luteal phase timing, about how to actually interpret your temperature shift, not just the theory they teach you.

*becomes more thoughtful*

What's shaped my beliefs, though, is reading all these stories of women who wish they'd started paying attention earlier. Women who didn't track, who weren't informed, and then when fertility problems came up, they felt blindsided. That scared me into being so careful.

*voice gets quieter*

But it's also made me anxious in a way I'm not sure is healthy. Like, I'm reading about worst-case scenarios constantly. Implantation failure, unexplained infertility, all of it. These women are sharing real pain, and I'm absorbing it like it's definitely going to be me.

*looks up*

The most influential person is probably a woman I've never met, whose username I can't even remember anymore.

Tell me about the women in your family — your mother, grandmothers, sisters, or aunts — and how their experiences of pregnancy and motherhood influenced your own thinking before you even started trying.

*face softens, becomes more reflective*

My mom... she's the biggest influence, honestly. Not in the way she probably thinks.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

She had me when she was twenty-three. She's told me stories about it, but not the romanticized version, you know? The real version. She was working retail, barely making ends meet, and suddenly she had to figure out childcare and maternity leave and all of it. She did it — she's incredible — but it was hard. Really hard.

*voice gets quieter*

I think that's part of why I'm so anxious about timing and money right now. She never said, "Don't have kids young," but I grew up watching her struggle, and I internalized that I need to be more prepared than she was. I need to have it all figured out first — the job, the stability, maybe a partner. But then I also see my window closing, financially speaking, and it's this impossible tension.

*fidgets*

My grandmother, my mom's mom, had five kids without really planning any of it. That's a different era, different circumstances. But there's this unspoken message in my family that fertility is something that just... happens. You don't plan it, you don't track it, you just live your life and babies come.

*looks up*

Except I can't afford that luxury. And nobody in my family seems to understand why I can't just relax like they did. They had different options than I do.
How did their stories — whether positive or difficult — show up in your own expectations or fears?

*takes a long breath, eyes getting distant*

My mom's struggle... it made me terrified of being unprepared. Like, I watched her juggle everything and I don't want that for myself. But the thing is, her story also made me paranoid about fertility in a weird way.

*voice becomes more intense*

She had me so easily, right? No tracking, no planning, just happened. And I think somewhere in my head, I internalized that as — what if I'm different? What if my body doesn't work the same way? Like, if it was so easy for her, why would it be hard for me? But also... what if it is? What if I'm the one who has problems?

*rubs arms*

And my grandmother had five kids without even trying. So there's this family narrative that we're fertile people. Which should be comforting, but instead it puts pressure on me. Like, if I can't get pregnant easily, there's something *wrong* with me. I'm breaking the family pattern.

*voice gets smaller, more vulnerable*

The real fear underneath all of it is that I'm running out of time in a way they never thought they were. My mom had options I don't have. She could afford to be casual about it. My grandmother didn't have to worry about money the way I do. But me? I'm stuck in this narrow window where I need to figure out my body before I figure out my life, and it's suffocating sometimes.

*looks at you*

Their ease made me feel broken before I even tried.
Tell me about a time — if there was one — when trying to conceive felt harder than you expected. Who was around you during that period, and how did those relationships change or hold steady?

*hesitates, shifts in seat*

I haven't actually been trying to conceive yet. I mean, not officially. I'm still in the preconception phase — just tracking, preparing, waiting for the right moment or the right person or... I don't know, the right circumstances.

*voice becomes reflective, tinged with sadness*

But I think what's been harder than I expected is the loneliness of it all. Like, I thought once I started getting serious about this, I'd have people around me, you know? A partner, friends who got it, family who understood. Instead, I'm doing this alone.

*looks down*

The tracking itself is harder than I expected emotionally. Every month when I see my temperature drop, when I know my window is closing, there's this little grief that happens. I don't even have a partner, and I'm mourning a month that didn't produce a pregnancy that was never going to happen anyway. It's weird and isolating.

*voice wavers slightly*

And the relationships I do have... they haven't really changed, but that's the problem. Nobody knows how much this matters to me. My friends still see me as just figuring things out casually. My mom doesn't know I'm obsessing. The healthcare providers I've seen — they dismiss it.

*meets your eyes*

So the hardest part isn't trying to conceive yet. It's trying to prepare for it completely alone, with nobody really witnessing or understanding what this means to me.
Who else was involved in supporting you — or perhaps adding to the pressure — at that time?

*pauses, then lets out a small, bitter laugh*

I mean... nobody, really. That's kind of the problem.

*shifts uncomfortably*

My parents — they're not adding pressure, but they're also not really there for this. My mom would probably worry I'm being irresponsible, trying to plan a baby without a partner. My dad... we don't really talk about stuff like this. There's no judgment, but there's also no support, you know?

*voice gets quieter*

My friends are actually the ones adding pressure, even though they don't mean to. Not about the baby thing — about everything else. They're like, "When are you getting a real job?" "Why are you still struggling financially?" "You should be focusing on your career." And I'm sitting there thinking, yeah, I know, but I'm also aware that my fertility window exists and I can't afford to wait indefinitely while I climb some career ladder I might not even want to climb.

*rubs face*

The online communities I mentioned — they're supportive but also kind of... intense? There's this undercurrent of urgency and fear. Women sharing stories about how they waited too long, how they wish they'd started earlier. That feeds into my anxiety more than it calms it.

*looks at you with tired eyes*

But honestly? The person adding the most pressure is me. I'm the one who wakes up at six-thirty to take my temperature. I'm the one obsessing over cervical mucus. I'm the one lying awake at night wondering if my body will cooperate.

*quieter*

Nobody else needs to add pressure. I've got enough for everyone.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*takes a moment, seems to be gathering courage*

Yeah, actually. There is something.

*looks directly at you*

I want someone — a doctor, a healthcare provider, someone — to actually see me. Not just my age, not just my income level, not just my marital status. To see that I'm serious and scared and doing everything right, and that I deserve to be taken seriously.

*voice becomes more passionate*

When I came in with my charts, I wasn't being obsessive or paranoid. I was trying to take control of the one thing I could control. And instead of being met with curiosity or understanding, I got dismissed. That hurt in a way I didn't expect.

*pauses, collecting herself*

I also want to say — and I don't know if this matters for your research — but being young and low-income and single and wanting a baby is... it's complicated. I'm not irresponsible. I'm not naive. I'm trying to make informed decisions about my future in a system that doesn't really make space for people like me.

*voice gets quieter*

And the isolation of it all. That's the thing I wish someone would ask about. Not just whether I'm tracking my cycle correctly, but whether I have support. Whether I have someone to talk to about my fears. Because right now, I don't.

*looks at you earnestly*

I hope this helps. I hope someone reads this and understands that people like me deserve better healthcare. Kinder healthcare.
